regional language.
(3) He mentions that Kfveda (sound produced by the mouth) is
popularly known as Sodanikd.2 We are informed that in the
Punjab and Malava people use the word in that sense.
(4) He says, similarly (under verse 41), that the hhd Bharadvdja is
called Ldfd by the people.
• (5) The word(which has already been noticed) occurring
in LXXXVII.33 was probably known to him as meaning
acrobats because of popular usage, though he does not give
any explanation. It looks like a vernacular or a foreign word,
(6') The word Cokfa* was understood to mean a Duffa or notorious
fellow, in popular parlance, according to Utpala, though the
dictionary gives just the contrary meaning i.e. ti is not
clear as to how this word came to connote the opposite sense.
Somebody has told us that the word Cokhd is used in the sense
of Sharp in Eastern Hind .
1. Vide LXVIII. 95.
2. „ LXXXVI. 39.
3. „ LXXXVII. 43.

xliv Brhat Samhita
